turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: The second case to be announced is Ziglar versus Abbasi and companion cases. After the September 11 terrorist attacks, many aliens in this country were detained and held in harsh conditions of confinement, pending a determination whether they had any connection to terrorism. Later, some of the aliens who had been detained, and they are the respondents here, filed suit. And the complaint named as the defendants, three high officers in the Department of Justice, as well as two wardens at the federal facility in New York where the detainees had been held. Those three high officials in the department and the wardens are the petitioners here. The respondents, the aliens who were held in confinement, alleged that they were detained in harsh conditions for a punitive purpose, that they were detained in those conditions because of their race, religion or national origin, that they were subject to arbitrary strip searches and that they were abused in various and serious ways by the facility's guards. Most of their claims sought damages pursuant to this Court's decision in Bivens versus Six Unknown Federal Narcotics Agents. Another claim was brought under 42 U.S.C's 1985(3) and this is the so called, conspiracy section of the Civil Rights Act of 1871. And the question presented here is whether the Court of Appeals erred by allowing all these claims to proceed past the motion to dismiss. In 1971, this Court held in Bivens, that despite the lack of specific statutory authorization, Courts could award damages for Fourth Amendment violations committed by federal officials. The Court later recognized and implied damages actions and these are so called, Bivens claims for two other constitutional violations. In Davis versus Passman, the Court allowed a secretary to sue a congressman for gender discrimination. And in Carlson versus Greene, the Court allowed a prisoner of state to sue federal jailers for failing to treat his asthma. These three cases represent the only instances in which the Court has approved and implied damages remedy under the constitution. Underpinning these three cases was the idea in another set of cases that Courts could apply causes of action to remedy violations of a person's rights under specific federal statutes. But that idea soon began to lose force. In a series of cases, the Court held that that the judiciary should not imply a private cause of action unless the statute itself shows an intention to create one. This change in the way the Court interpreted statutes led to a change in the Bivens doctrine as well. For when a party seeks to assert and imply cause of action under the constitution separation of powers' principles are central and the question in essence is, who should decide whether to provide for a damages remedy, Congress or the Courts? The answer most often would be Congress. As a result, the Court has urged caution before extending the Bivens remedy into any new context. And the Court's precedents make clear that the Bivens remedy will not be available if there are special factors counseling hesitation. When determining whether those special factors exist in the case, the question is whether there are sound reasons to think Congress might doubt the necessity of a damages remedy is part of the system for protecting constitutional rights. With respect to respondents' claims challenging the confinement imposed pursuant to the formal policy, the Court concludes that there are special factors counseling hesitation here. As an initial manner, the claims against the high executive officials would require inquiry into the functioning near the executive branch at the highest levels. That inquiry would prevent officials from devoting time and effort to the proper discharge of their duties. Furthermore, there are three other special factors that apply even to the claim was brought against the wardens. First, the claim's challenge major elements of the nation's response to this September 11 attack, thus requiring inquiry into sensitive areas on National Security Policy, which is the prerogative of the Congress and the President rather than the courts. Judicial inquiry into the National Security realm raises serious separation of powers concerns. These concerns are even more pronounced when the claim seeks damages for the risk of personal financial liability might cause an official to second guess difficult but necessary decisions concerning National Security policy. Second, Congress has not legislated in the area despite the frequent and intense attention that has been devoted to the allegations at issue here. Finally, there might well have been other remedies available to respondents, in particular, injunctive relief or writs of habeas corpus and these too, makes it less likely the Congress would deem a damages action is necessary. For all these reasons, the Court of Appeals should not have allowed most of the respondents' Bivens claims to proceed past the motion to dismiss. One of the Bivens claims, however, requires a different analysis. This is the claim alleging that the facility's wardens allowed the guards to abuse the detainees. The Court of Appeals held that this claim required no extension of Bivens and for this reason, it held that no analysis or special factors was necessary. Now, let there be no doubt if these allegations are true, the prison's abuses were serious, and it is also true that this claim does have significant parallels to the Court's earlier decision in Carlson involving denial of medical care. There, the Court authorized damages for failure to treat a prisoner's medical condition. Even so, there are still meaningful differences between this claim and that one. Unlike Carlson, this claim arises under the Fifth Amendment rather than the Eighth Amendment, and the detainee here -- detainees here might have helped alternative -- remedies with which to secure relief from the alleged abuse. And Congress has not provided a standalone damage remedy for prisoner abuse as the federal officials, even though it made significant changes to the way prison abuse claims against state officials must be brought when it passed the Prison Litigation Reform Act. In light of these circumstances, the Court of Appeals should have performed a special factors analysis before allowing this Bivens action to proceed. Although this Court could perform the special factors analysis in the first instance, the parties have not much concentrated on the prisoner abuse claims in their briefs and arguments in these cases, and the Court therefore, vacates the judgment with respect to that claim. A special factors analysis should be performed in the first instance on remand. Respondents' one remaining claim is the petitioners are liable for Civil Conspiracy under 1995(3). That statute imposes liability on persons who conspire to deprive any person or class of persons of the equal protection of the laws. Petitioners contend that they are entitled to qualified immunity with respect to this claim. For reasons explained in the opinion, it is not clear that the statute applies to conspiracy allegations against officials who were all in the same department. In light of these uncertainties, petitioners are entitled to qualified immunity under the conspiracy statute so, those claims against petitioners must be dismissed. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is reversed as to all of the claims except the prisoner abuse claim. The judgment of the Court of Appeals in respect that claim is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Ginsburg has joined. Justice Sotomayor, Justice Kagan and Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration and decision of these cases.
